facts: A stellar-mass black hole has a gravity 1.3 million times stronger than Earth's
If a black hole with the mass of the Sun were placed at the center of our solar system, its event horizon would be about 1.9 million miles across, roughly 3 times the distance from NY to LA
The point of no return around a supermassive black hole is about 12 million kilometers in diameter, roughly the size of the orbit of Mercury
idea.payoff: and we still don't know what lies beyond the event horizon
idea.title: Black Hole Gravity 🌌
idea.description: Imagine a force so strong, not even light can escape 🌟. Black holes are the cosmic extremes of gravity, warping space and time in extreme ways. But what secrets lie beyond the point of no return, and will we ever uncover them? 🤔

scenes.visual: Dark space background with twinkling stars. A faint, swirling cosmic dust cloud forms a question mark.
Earth icon on the left, shrinking slightly. A small, glowing black hole icon appears on the right, growing larger. Gravitational pull lines emanate from the black hole.

scenes.text: Sun-mass BH event horizon: 1.9M miles. (3x NY to LA)
scenes.visual: A stylized Sun, which then shrinks to a central black hole. A large, faint circular boundary (event horizon) expands around it. A simplified map showing NY and LA appears, then a 'x3' multiplier fades in.

scenes.text: Supermassive BH 'no return': 12M km. (Size of Mercury's orbit)
scenes.visual: A vast, swirling supermassive black hole at the center. A faint orbit path appears around it, illustrating the size comparison to Mercury's orbit.

scenes.text:
Beyond the event horizon...
WE STILL DON'T KNOW.
scenes.visual: Deep, mysterious black hole. The event horizon shimmers, then darkens into an unknown abyss. Faint question marks emerge from the darkness.
